How do you train AI agents that can use computers like humans? 

MIT CSAIL researchers introduce "OSGym": scalable OS infrastructure to improve the capabilities of computer use agents. It introduces large-scale training made possible by extensive infrastructure optimization:

So why did the researchers develop OSGym?

Training computer use agents is both a modeling problem & an infrastructure challenge:

• Each task needs a full OS (GUI, apps, browser, etc.)
• Scaling → massive CPU/RAM/disk costs
• Random crashes from real software environments
•

The key insight behind OSGym: Don’t scale like traditional systems.

• Decentralize each OS replica (no global bottleneck)
• Shift from CPU-bound → RAM-bound scaling (10x cheaper)
• Use XFS reflink copy-on-write disks (37x faster disk provisioning)

The result: Scalable,

graphify turns code, docs, PDFs, and screenshots into a structured graph. No vector DB, no embeddings. Just graph topology with Leiden clustering. Open source, runs locally, works as a Claude Code skill.

Two-pass

71.5x fewer tokens per query vs reading raw files on a 52-file corpus. Persistent graph.json means no reprocessing. --watch mode rebuilds as files change. Git hooks rebuild on every commit and branch switch. Exports to interactive HTML, JSON, Markdown, SVG, GraphML, and Neo4j.

One-line install: pip install graphifyy && graphify install

Then /graphify . to process your current directory. Query concepts, trace paths between files, explain architectural decisions.
